content.text: Why Disclosure Timelines Are a Trust Mechanism

Disclosure timelines serve a dual purpose. For the security researcher, they are a commitment that their finding will be acted on within a defined window — after which they are free to publish. For your organisation, they create the scheduling pressure that keeps remediation from slipping indefinitely into the engineering backlog. Without a published timeline, both parties are negotiating in ambiguity at every step.

The 90-day standard originates from Google Project Zero's 2013 policy. The rationale: 90 days is sufficient time to patch most vulnerabilities in software products; it is long enough to be fair to the vendor; and it is short enough that the researcher's incentive to publish remains intact. In practice, AI-powered applications often need shorter timelines for critical vulnerabilities because prompt injection and tool-call abuse can be actively exploited in ways that conventional web vulnerabilities cannot while a patch is still in progress.

A disclosure timeline is not a deadline imposed by the researcher — it is an interval agreed to when the VDP policy was published. By accepting a report through your VDP channel, you implicitly accept the timeline commitments stated in that policy. Disputing them after the fact damages trust in a way that is very difficult to repair.
Severity-Based Timeline Reference

attrs.alt: Gantt-style timeline showing acknowledgement, patch, coordinated disclosure window, and public disclosure dates by severity: Critical (24h), High (7 days), Medium (30 days), Low (90 days)

content.text: Defining Each Phase of the Timeline
Phase 1: Acknowledgement

Acknowledgement is the receipt confirmation that confirms scope check has begun. It is not a triage decision — it is proof that the report was received and is being processed by a human. Automated receipt emails count for this purpose, as long as they include a ticket ID and a stated next-step timeline.
Severity
Acknowledgement SLA
What It Must Include
Critical
1 hour (automated) + 24h (human confirmation of triage start)
Ticket ID; statement that critical-severity triage is under way; escalation contact
High
1 hour (automated) + 48h (human)
Ticket ID; severity assessment timeline
Medium / Low
1 hour (automated) + 5–10 business days (human)
Ticket ID; triage timeline statement
Informational
1 hour (automated) + 15 business days
Ticket ID; note that informational findings may be accepted as-is with no patch
Phase 2: Remediation Window

The remediation window is the time between initial report and patch deployment. It is the window your engineering team has to fix the issue. Several factors routinely extend this beyond the target SLA:
Root cause complexity.
A single-file input sanitisation fix may take hours. A systemic prompt injection vulnerability that requires architectural changes to the tool-call pipeline may take weeks.
Deployment cadence.
Even a completed fix may not reach production if your deployment process requires QA cycles, staged rollouts, or change advisory board approval.
Downstream dependencies.
If the vulnerability is in a shared library or third-party component, your fix timeline is constrained by the upstream maintainer's patch schedule.
Supply chain scope.
If the vulnerability affects multiple customers who self-host your Claude integration, the patch timeline must account for customer update lag, not just your own deployment.
Phase 3: Coordinated Disclosure Window
Once the patch is deployed, the coordinated disclosure window begins. This is the buffer between patch availability and public announcement, during which:
Your customers and downstream users have time to apply the patch before an exploit is published.
You draft and review the security advisory.
The researcher reviews the advisory for accuracy and approves the acknowledgement section.
A CVE is requested and assigned if appropriate.
The disclosure date is confirmed with the researcher.

The coordinated disclosure window is typically 7–14 days for high-severity issues. For critical issues that are actively exploitable, the window may be as short as 24–48 hours — the priority is getting the patch deployed, not waiting for perfect coordination.
Requesting and Granting Timeline Extensions

Extension requests are a normal part of the VDP process for genuinely complex vulnerabilities. The key is that they must be negotiated — not unilaterally declared. A vendor who silently misses a deadline has broken the agreement; a vendor who proactively requests an extension with a concrete new date and a credible explanation is acting in good faith.
Conditions That Justify an Extension Request
The vulnerability has supply-chain scope and requires coordination with multiple affected vendors through CERT/CC or a similar body
A patch exists but requires a dependency update that itself has a pending release from an upstream maintainer
The fix requires a major architectural change that cannot be safely deployed without extended QA
A regulatory notification obligation is triggered and the notification timeline must be completed before public disclosure
Extension Request Template

Subject: Re: [VDP-{ticket_id}] Timeline extension request

Hi {researcher_name},

We are writing to request an extension to the disclosure timeline for VDP-{ticket_id}.

Current agreed disclosure date: {original_date}
Requested new disclosure date: {new_date} ({N} additional days)

Reason for the request:
{Specific, factual explanation — e.g., "The fix requires updating a dependency
(libXYZ v2.3) which the upstream maintainer has scheduled for release on {date}.
We need 7 additional days after that release to complete integration testing
before we can safely deploy to production."}

What we have completed so far:
- {Root cause identified and documented}
- {Fix developed and in QA}
- {N customers notified of pending patch}

We will provide a status update on {intermediate_date} and confirm the new
target date at that time.

If this extension is not acceptable, we are prepared to discuss alternative
approaches including a partial disclosure of the advisory without technical
details while the full fix is deployed.

Please reply to confirm whether you agree to the extension.

— [Organisation] Security Team
What to Do If the Researcher Refuses an Extension

If the researcher declines an extension request, you have limited options. You cannot legally compel them to delay disclosure if they are acting within the VDP policy's stated timelines. Options in order of preference:
Deploy an incomplete mitigating control (rate limit, input validation, feature flag disabling the affected component) before the disclosure date to reduce the immediate exploit risk.
Publish a partial advisory on the original date acknowledging the vulnerability and stating that a full patch is pending, with the patch date committed.
Negotiate a partial disclosure: the researcher publishes a description of the vulnerability class without a proof-of-concept, pending patch availability.
If the vulnerability is actively being exploited in the wild, emergency patch immediately — the disclosure date becomes irrelevant if exploitation is already public.
SLA Tracking and Escalation Automation

import anthropic, json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SEVERITY_SLAS = {
    "critical":      {"ack_hours": 24,   "patch_days": 1,  "disclose_days": 2},
    "high":          {"ack_hours": 48,   "patch_days": 7,  "disclose_days": 14},
    "medium":        {"ack_hours": 120,  "patch_days": 30, "disclose_days": 60},
    "low":           {"ack_hours": 240,  "patch_days": 90, "disclose_days": 90},
    "informational": {"ack_hours": 360,  "patch_days": 90, "disclose_days": 90},
}

def compute_milestone_dates(report_date: str, severity: str) -> dict:
    """Compute SLA milestone dates from a report submission date and severity."""
    sla = SEVERITY_SLAS[severity.lower()]
    base = datetime.fromisoformat(report_date)
    return {
        "report_date": report_date,
        "severity": severity,
        "ack_deadline": (base + timedelta(hours=sla["ack_hours"])).isoformat(),
        "patch_deadline": (base + timedelta(days=sla["patch_days"])).isoformat(),
        "disclosure_deadline": (base + timedelta(days=sla["disclose_days"])).isoformat(),
    }

def generate_sla_status_report(tickets: list[dict]) -> str:
    """
    Produce a natural-language SLA status summary for a list of open tickets,
    highlighting any breached or at-risk milestones.
    """
    now = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    prompt = f"""You are a VDP operations analyst. Today is {now} UTC.
Review the following open VDP tickets and their milestone dates.
Produce a concise status report (bullet list) identifying:
1. Any tickets where a deadline has already passed (breach)
2. Any tickets where a deadline is within 48 hours (at risk)
3. An overall programme health assessment (1 sentence)

Tickets:
{json.dumps(tickets, indent=2)}"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=768,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


# Example
tickets = [
    {**compute_milestone_dates("2024-06-25T09:00:00", "critical"), "ticket_id": "VDP-2024-091", "title": "Tool-call chain arbitrary file read", "patch_status": "deployed"},
    {**compute_milestone_dates("2024-06-28T14:00:00", "high"),     "ticket_id": "VDP-2024-093", "title": "Indirect prompt injection via PDF upload", "patch_status": "in_development"},
    {**compute_milestone_dates("2024-07-01T10:00:00", "medium"),   "ticket_id": "VDP-2024-097", "title": "Rate limit bypass on /api/v1/chat", "patch_status": "triaged"},
]
print(generate_sla_status_report(tickets))
Industry Benchmarks and Regulatory Alignment
Disclosure timeline norms are converging across the industry. Understanding where your SLAs sit relative to these benchmarks helps you set defensible policies and respond to researcher challenges.
Organisation / Standard
Critical
High
Medium / Low
Extension Policy
Google Project Zero
90 days (no severity split)
90 days
90 days
14-day grace for active patching; no further extensions
CERT/CC
45 days default
45 days
45 days
Extensions case-by-case for complex supply-chain issues
ISO/IEC 30111 (Vulnerability Handling)
Recommends 'as soon as possible'
No specific number
No specific number
Emphasises good-faith coordination over fixed dates
EU Cyber Resilience Act (2027)
24h to ENISA; patch 'without undue delay'
To be specified in implementing acts
To be specified
Regulatory timeline overrides VDP timeline for notifiable incidents
Anthropic's own VDP
See Anthropic's published policy
See Anthropic's published policy
See Anthropic's published policy
Coordinate with Anthropic for findings that affect their infrastructure

The EU Cyber Resilience Act introduces mandatory vulnerability notification timelines for products with digital elements. If your Claude application is distributed as a product (not purely SaaS), you may have statutory notification obligations to ENISA that run parallel to — and override — your VDP timeline commitments.
When Active Exploitation Changes Everything

Standard disclosure timelines assume the vulnerability is not yet being actively exploited. When exploitation is confirmed in the wild — whether reported by the researcher, observed in your own logs, or disclosed by a third party — the timeline collapses to near-zero:
Immediately escalate to your incident response process — VDP coordination becomes secondary to containment.
Deploy any available mitigating control within hours, even if incomplete (disable the feature, add IP allowlisting, reduce model capabilities in the affected deployment).
Notify affected customers immediately, before the patch is ready, so they can implement compensating controls.
Agree with the researcher on emergency disclosure — typically 24–48 hours — or, if the researcher is already prepared to publish, facilitate coordinated simultaneous publication.
Issue a CVE the same day if the vulnerability affects third-party deployments.
Partial and Staged Disclosure Strategies

Binary disclosure — either fully secret or fully public — is rarely optimal. Several intermediate disclosure strategies reduce the risk window while giving your organisation additional time to complete remediation:
Limited Disclosure to Affected Customers

For vulnerabilities that affect a defined set of enterprise customers running your Claude integration in self-hosted environments, you can notify those customers privately before public disclosure. This gives them time to apply compensating controls or schedule patch deployment without exposing the vulnerability to the broader attack surface.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_customer_pre_disclosure_notice(vulnerability: dict, customer_context: dict) -> str:
    """
    Generate a tailored pre-disclosure notice for a specific customer,
    given the vulnerability details and their deployment context.
    """
    prompt = f"""You are a security communications specialist. Write a pre-disclosure notice
for a high-severity vulnerability in a Claude-powered application.

Vulnerability:
{json.dumps(vulnerability, indent=2)}

Customer context:
- Company: {customer_context["company"]}
- Deployment type: {customer_context["deployment_type"]}
- Claude integration version: {customer_context["version"]}
- Affected: {customer_context["is_affected"]}

The notice should:
1. State clearly that this is a CONFIDENTIAL pre-disclosure communication (embargoed until {vulnerability["public_disclosure_date"]})
2. Describe the vulnerability in technical terms sufficient for their security team to assess risk
3. State whether their specific deployment version is affected
4. List the available mitigations they can apply immediately, before the patch
5. State the patch availability date and how they will receive it
6. Provide a dedicated contact channel for questions
7. Be under 400 words; professional but not alarmist

Do not include exploit code or proof-of-concept details."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=768,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


vuln = {
    "id": "VDP-2024-093",
    "title": "Indirect prompt injection via PDF document upload",
    "severity": "High",
    "cvss_score": "8.1",
    "affected_versions": ["1.4.0", "1.4.1", "1.4.2"],
    "patch_version": "1.4.3",
    "patch_available_date": "2024-07-10",
    "public_disclosure_date": "2024-07-17",
    "mitigation": "Disable PDF upload feature or validate documents against an allowlist of trusted sources before passing to Claude context"
}

customer = {
    "company": "Acme Financial Services",
    "deployment_type": "self-hosted",
    "version": "1.4.1",
    "is_affected": True
}

print(generate_customer_pre_disclosure_notice(vuln, customer))
Advisory-First, Exploit-Later Disclosure

When a patch is not yet fully deployed but the disclosure window has arrived, publishing the advisory without the proof-of-concept is a defensible middle ground. The researcher publishes a description of the vulnerability class and impact; the proof-of-concept is held until the patch has reached a defined adoption threshold (e.g., 80% of known deployments, or 30 days post-patch, whichever comes first).

This approach requires explicit agreement with the researcher before the disclosure date. It is not appropriate if the researcher has indicated they will publish in full regardless — in that case, your advisory must be ready to publish simultaneously.
Coordinated Multi-Vendor Disclosure

When a vulnerability affects multiple vendors using the same Claude integration pattern (e.g., a shared open-source RAG library that passes retrieved content directly to Claude's context), coordinated multi-vendor disclosure through CERT/CC or similar bodies is the appropriate process. Key characteristics:
A single coordinating body (CERT/CC, NCSC, or similar) manages communication with all affected vendors simultaneously
A shared embargo date is agreed — all vendors patch and disclose on the same day
The originating researcher is credited in all advisories
A single CVE is assigned, covering all affected implementations
The timeline for multi-vendor coordination is typically 90 days regardless of severity, because synchronising patch cycles across multiple engineering organisations requires longer lead time
Writing Effective Security Advisories for AI Vulnerabilities

A security advisory for a prompt injection or model manipulation vulnerability requires different framing than a conventional web vulnerability advisory. The technical audience needs to understand not just what was vulnerable but how the AI's behaviour was altered and what the blast radius of exploitation looks like.
Advisory Section
Conventional Web Vuln
AI-Specific Addition
Summary
Affected product, version, severity, impact
Also state: whether the vulnerability is probabilistic vs. deterministic; whether it requires user interaction or is fully automated
Vulnerability details
Root cause (e.g., missing input validation); CWE ID
Also state: the injection vector (direct, indirect via retrieval, tool output); how Claude's behaviour changes; what attacker-controlled content looks like
Impact
Data exposure, privilege escalation, code execution
Also state: which tool calls could be triggered; what data in the context window is at risk; whether the operator system prompt was exposed
Proof of concept
HTTP request / response pair or code snippet
Representative prompt payload — but withhold payloads that could be directly weaponised; describe the technique class instead
Remediation
Version to patch to; configuration change
Also state: whether model-level controls (Anthropic's safety layers) provide any defence; what input validation or context isolation changes are required in the application layer
Common Timeline Failures and Recovery Patterns
Missed patch deadline with no communication.

Recovery: contact the researcher immediately with a sincere apology, a concrete new date (not a range), and a concrete mitigation available today. Silence after a missed deadline is the worst possible response — researchers interpret it as confirmation that the organisation has no intention of patching.
Patch deployed but advisory delayed.

Recovery: the advisory can be published by the researcher on the original disclosure date even if you have not yet published yours. To avoid this, begin drafting the advisory in parallel with patch development, not after patch deployment.
CVE requested late.

Recovery: a CVE can be requested after public disclosure (retroactive assignment). It is preferable to have it before, but late assignment does not invalidate the advisory. Use MITRE's CNA submission process.
Customer notification missed.

Recovery: notify affected customers immediately on discovery of the miss. Do not wait for the public disclosure date — if the patch is not yet deployed, customers need time to implement compensating controls. A same-day customer notification after a delay is still far better than no notification.
Researcher publishes before agreed date.

Recovery: publish your advisory immediately, regardless of readiness. A partial advisory is better than no advisory when a researcher has already described the vulnerability publicly. Document the timeline breach in your VDP records — if the researcher was acting contrary to good faith, this documentation may be relevant to future legal analysis.
Summary

Disclosure timelines are the contractual foundation of a VDP — they give researchers confidence that their findings will be acted on and give your organisation the structure to prioritise remediation. For Claude applications, severity-based SLAs should range from 24 hours for critical findings to 90 days for low-severity issues, with explicit coordinated disclosure windows after patch deployment. SLA tracking automation surfaces breached and at-risk milestones before they cause coordination failures. Extensions should be negotiated proactively, with concrete new dates and credible technical rationale. Active exploitation collapses all timelines to near-zero and transfers control to the incident response process.
